The Chinese Tourist Gambit: Why The Marcos Administration Is Courting Beijing Amid Lessons Unlearned

The short answer coming from government is that it is trying to balance economic pressure, regional competition, and the hard reality that our tourism recovery is lagging behind our neighbors. But critics and security analysts worry that we are falling back into old habits, ignoring the hard lessons of the Duterte years in exchange for quick arrival numbers that might not even materialize.

## The Numbers Tell A Complex Story

First, the numbers show that Chinese tourists are not the ones dominating our arrivals right now. In 2025, the Philippines recorded a total of 6.48 million international visitors. That is a decent number, but it is still far from the pre-pandemic peak of 8.26 million in 2019.

Within that 6.48 million, Chinese tourists only accounted for 262,144 visitors, putting them in sixth place. Compare that to 2019, when China was our second-largest market with 1.74 million visitors. That is an 85 percent drop.

## South Korea's Alarming Decline

If you are looking for a real tourism crisis, you should look at South Korea, not China. In 2025, South Korea remained our top market, but the numbers were deeply worrying. We had 1.62 million Korean visitors, which sounds like a lot until you realize it is 20 percent lower than 2019 levels. Even worse, the monthly arrivals for the last few months of 2025 actually fell by 2.2 percent year-on-year, which is the opposite direction of the regional trend.

That decline is driven by a mix of factors mostly outside the control of our Department of Tourism: the weakening of the South Korean won, rising domestic travel costs within Korea, and a shift in Korean travel preferences toward Japan and Vietnam.

The United States sits in second place with about 1.06 million visitors, contributing around 17 percent of our total arrivals. Japan is third with 498,000, and Australia is fourth with 312,000.

All of that makes it clear that Chinese tourists are just one piece of a much larger tourism picture, yet they are getting a disproportionate amount of policy attention.

## Still Below Pre-Pandemic Levels

If you trace the story of our arrivals from 2019 to 2025, it goes like this. In 2019, we hit the high-water mark of 8.26 million. Then the pandemic happened, and numbers crashed to almost zero. In 2022, we started to recover with 2.65 million. In 2023, we hit 5.45 million. In 2024, it was 5.92 million. And finally, 6.48 million in 2025.

So even after all the reopening and all the campaigns, we are still about 29 percent below where we were seven years ago.

## Why China, Why Now?

The 14-day visa-free policy for Chinese citizens is being framed by the government not as a political move, but as a "catch-up" move. Thailand, Malaysia, and Singapore have all implemented visa-free or very easy visa policies for Chinese tourists over the last two years, and they have seen a massive surge in arrivals as a result.

Before this policy, Chinese travelers to the Philippines had to undergo a slower and often clogged electronic visa process or apply through travel agencies. It was a friction point that made many Chinese tourists simply choose Thailand or Vietnam instead.

Now, the Philippines is trying to catch up. From January 16, 2026 to January 15, 2027, Chinese passport holders can enter the Philippines for up to 14 days without a visa, provided they arrive at Ninoy Aquino International Airport (NAIA) in Manila or Mactan-Cebu International Airport.

The government and business groups are hoping that this will push Chinese arrivals toward around 1.2 million for 2026, which would represent a massive jump from the 262,000 we saw in 2025.

Some Filipino-Chinese business groups have warmly welcomed the policy, calling it forward-looking. They argue that tourism is the "low-hanging fruit" for the economy and that we shouldn't let geopolitical disputes kill our economic opportunities.

For Chinese companies and investors, the policy means they no longer have to wait weeks for visa approvals to check on their projects or meet partners. In that sense, it is as much about business as it is about vacationers.

## Not A Blank Check: Policy Limits

On paper, this is not an unlimited opening. It is a one-year pilot. It is restricted to just two airports to make monitoring easier. It requires travelers to have a return ticket and a passport valid for at least six months. And the government has stressed that the Bureau of Immigration and the intelligence community will be keeping a close eye on any red flags.

In other words, the official line is that we are opening the door slightly, in specific places, under heavy guard.

## Why The Timing Feels Wrong

The big problem is timing. The visa-free policy was rolled out with very little public discussion at a time when tensions with Beijing are at an all-time high.

There was a violent confrontation in June 2024 where a Filipino soldier lost part of his thumb. In August and September 2025, there were more ramming incidents and the use of water cannons against Philippine Coast Guard vessels near Sabina Shoal and Second Thomas Shoal.

So when the government suddenly announces that Chinese citizens can now enter Manila and Cebu without a visa, many Filipinos are asking: "Why are we being so nice to them while they are being so aggressive toward us?"

## The Duterte "China Problem": Lessons Learned And Ignored

To understand the anxiety around this new policy, we have to look back at Duterte's legacy with China, which many see as a cautionary tale of "lessons unlearned."

## What Duterte Got Wrong

First, there was the secret "gentleman's agreement" with Xi Jinping over the West Philippine Sea. Duterte essentially agreed to keep the status quo, which in practice meant allowing China to continue building and militarizing while we stayed quiet. This backfired spectacularly as China used that time to solidify its control.

Second, Duterte leaned heavily into China's promises of infrastructure loans and investments under the "Build, Build, Build" program. Most of those promises never happened. Out of dozens of major projects promised by Beijing, only a handful were ever completed, and even those came with high interest rates and Chinese labor.

Third, Duterte's government opened the door wide to Philippine Offshore Gaming Operators or POGOs, which were almost entirely funded and staffed by Chinese nationals. While they brought in some revenue, they also brought a wave of crime—kidnappings, human trafficking, money laundering, and even suspected espionage. It took years for the Marcos administration to finally ban them in late 2024.

Duterte's broader stance on the West Philippine Sea was also very clear. He repeatedly said he would not go to war with China and often brushed aside the 2016 Hague ruling that invalidated China's sweeping claims. He prioritized the promise of loans, investments, and later vaccines over a more assertive defense of our maritime rights. That created a situation where we seemed too dependent on China's goodwill, without getting a fair return.

## What Marcos Has Actually Changed

To be fair, Marcos has tried to walk a different path.

He decided to move the Philippines away from China's Belt and Road Initiative and instead lean more toward partners like the United States and Japan for infrastructure. His administration finally pulled the plug on POGOs, something Duterte loudly refused to do. He expanded our defense cooperation with the U.S. by approving more EDCA sites and increasing joint military exercises, strengthening the security side of our foreign relations.

On the West Philippine Sea, Marcos has taken a firmer line. He has been more vocal in saying that the Philippines will not give up any territory and has publicly backed Filipino officials who speak out against Chinese aggression. When China complained about statements by Philippine Coast Guard spokesperson Jay Tarriela, Marcos said he stands by his officials and wants them to tell the truth to the Filipino people about what is happening at sea. The National Security Council also reminded Beijing that our agencies do not need any foreign country's approval to talk to Filipinos.

So in that sense, yes, the Marcos administration has stepped away from Duterte's habit of accommodating China at all costs. But at the same time, it still has to face economic realities. China remains one of our largest trading partners. Our tourism recovery is slow, and we cannot afford to completely shut out a huge potential tourism market. That is the tension: we want to be politically tougher, but we are still economically open.

## Christina Frasco's Performance: Underfunded, Not Underperforming?

Christina Garcia Frasco's time at the Department of Tourism has been noisy: rebranding controversies, budget fights, and now this China visa-free policy. But a closer look at her record shows a mix of technical wins and political missteps.

## The Budget Story

DOT's promotional budget has been slashed hard:
In 2023, the department had PHP 1.2 billion for promotions. In 2024, that was cut to PHP 200 million. In 2025, it was raised slightly but remained well below historic levels.

Before the pandemic, DOT usually had more than PHP 1 billion a year to promote the country. So Frasco has been trying to market the Philippines with about 15 to 20 percent of the budget her predecessors had.

Despite that, Frasco points out that in 2024, with just PHP 200 million in promotional money, tourism still contributed 8.6 percent to the GDP. She calls this "doing more with less."

Some lawmakers and industry observers have suggested the budget cuts were not just about fiscal discipline, but were a way of punishing Frasco for the "Love the Philippines" campaign disaster.

## What DOT Has Done Right

Even with such a small budget, DOT under Frasco has not been idle. In 2025, the department helped organize over 200 festivals and events nationwide to boost domestic travel.

On the branding side, the Michelin Guide finally entered the Philippine market, listing 108 restaurants and giving the country's food scene a massive international boost.

One major policy win was the implementation of the VAT refund mechanism for non-resident tourists under Republic Act 12028. This makes the Philippines more competitive with shopping hubs like Singapore and Thailand.

The department rolled out Tourist Rest Areas around the country, set up a 24/7 Tourist Assistance Center, and launched a multi-lingual app. These are the "boring" but necessary parts of tourism infrastructure that actually make a difference to travelers.

For 2026, DOT's strategy is to focus hard on recovering the South Korean market, deepening already strong ties with the U.S. and Canada, and aggressively targeting India, which is seeing a massive surge in outbound travelers.

## The Metrics That Actually Matter

While lawmakers and commentators obsess over arrival numbers, Frasco and her team argue that other numbers tell a better story.

Tourism revenue in 2024 hit PHP 760.5 billion, an all-time record and about 126 percent recovery compared to 2019. This means that even though we have fewer tourists, they are staying longer and spending more per person.

The Philippines also has the largest domestic tourism market in Southeast Asia, valued at around $54 billion. This domestic strength is what kept the industry alive during the pandemic and continues to provide a safety net today.

On the governance side, the Department of Tourism has repeatedly passed Commission on Audit scrutiny with "unqualified opinions" for 2023 and 2024, and it earned ISO 9001:2015 certification for its quality management systems.

## Public Approval Versus Political Attacks

Here is where things get strange. In May 2024, Frasco received a 92 percent approval rating in the RP-Mission and Development Foundation (RPMD) survey. By late 2025, her rating was still high at 89 percent.

So whatever the noise on social media and in congressional hearings, the general public does not seem to think she is doing a bad job.

Yet at the same time, two lawmakers, Representatives Jude Acidre and Paolo Ortega, have publicly attacked her performance, calling for her replacement and describing her as a "liability" to the administration.

In early January 2026, rumors spread that Malacañang was considering replacing her with a corporate executive from a major aviation and property conglomerate.

## What Frasco Has Mishandled

At the same time, there are clear missteps that belong to Frasco and her team, and these cannot be ignored.

The "Love the Philippines" campaign is the biggest one. In 2023, DOT launched this new slogan and campaign video, only for people to discover that the video used stock footage from other countries like Indonesia, Brazil, and the United Arab Emirates. It was a global embarrassment for a country whose main selling point is its own natural beauty.

Senator Nancy Binay, who chairs the Senate tourism committee, called the campaign "unsalvageable" and "fatally flawed."

But Frasco chose to keep the "Love the Philippines" slogan despite Binay and others calling it "beautiful but broken."

Then there is the issue of self-promotion. Many people have complained that DOT's materials and events focus too much on Frasco herself rather than the destinations. Critics point to social media posts and event banners where her image is larger or more prominent than the sights of the Philippines.

In ABS-CBN and ANC interviews in January 2026, Frasco pushed back hard, calling the allegation "a coordinated political attack" and "baseless."

## Things Beyond Her Control

Lawmakers and commentators have compared our roughly 5 to 6 million tourist arrivals with Thailand's 30 million or Vietnam's 15 million. But comparing the Philippines to Thailand is not an apples-to-apples comparison.

Bank of America Global Research called the Philippines "a clear laggard" in a 2024 report, pointing out that our neighbors have land borders that allow for easy travel. You can drive from China to Vietnam, or from Malaysia to Thailand. To get to the Philippines, almost everyone has to fly.

The fall in South Korean arrivals has more to do with the Korean won, domestic politics in Korea, and a shift in where Koreans want to go (mostly Japan and Vietnam) than it does with any Philippine marketing campaign.

International media have picked this up. The Straits Times ran a piece in early 2026 describing how the Philippines is being "squeezed" between regional giants like Thailand and the rising star of Vietnam.

So in the end, Frasco is both someone who has made poor judgments in certain high-profile cases and someone who is being blamed for structural problems that were there long before she took office.

## The Aviation Reform Angle

An Inquirer opinion piece titled "The Frasco story: Friction created by reform" offers a different lens on why the attacks keep coming. The column argues that Frasco has publicly identified "genuine barriers to growth: connectivity issues, exorbitant airfare, aviation market framework that favors established players." The piece says that "for vocalizing what the industry has long whispered in private, she faces repercussions."

According to this view, when Frasco challenged aviation monopolies and pricing power, she posed a threat to powerful business arrangements. The opinion writer warns of "whispers regarding possible conflicts of interest—with individuals from the aviation industry eyeing positions in tourism" and describes the attacks on her as "a strategy to undermine reform advocates without addressing policy."

The column urges readers to "support those who advocate for transparency and competition" and to "refuse to let cynical narratives obstruct reforms."

This framing does not erase the branding fiasco or the self-promotion issue, but it suggests that some of the "poor job" narrative is not organic outrage from tourists. Instead, it may be pushback from players who do not want DOT poking at pricing power and monopolistic practices in the airline and aviation sectors. If true, this would mean Frasco is being punished not just for her mistakes but also for threatening entrenched business interests.

## The Contradictions In Frasco's Record

Taken all together, Frasco's performance is full of contradictions that make simple judgments difficult.

She has a 92 percent public approval rating in mid-2024 and 89 percent in late 2025, yet she faces congressional attacks and persistent replacement rumors.

She presides over record tourism revenue of PHP 760.5 billion and the highest per-capita tourist spending we've ever seen, yet arrival numbers remain 29 percent below 2019 levels.

These contradictions point to a more complex story than either "Frasco is doing great" or "Frasco is a failure."

She is technically competent. The audits, the ISO certification, the revenue numbers, the employment growth in the sector (6.21 million jobs in 2023) all suggest a department that is functioning well at a structural level.

But she is politically clumsy. The "Love the Philippines" disaster, the decision to keep the slogan, and the optics of her self-promotion have given her enemies all the ammunition they need to make her look like a liability.

She is also caught in forces beyond her control. The Chinese tourist collapse, the South Korean market decline, and the high cost of air travel are not things she can fix with a new logo.

And she may be caught in a political and business crossfire. If the Inquirer opinion piece is correct, she is being attacked because she is trying to reform the aviation system that makes Philippine travel so expensive.

## So Is Frasco Part Of The Problem?

The honest answer is yes, but only in specific ways.

She is part of the problem when it comes to judgment, optics, and political skill. The "Love the Philippines" campaign was a massive unforced error that damaged the country's brand.

But she is not the problem when it comes to the structural reasons why Philippine tourism lags behind Thailand and Vietnam. She didn't build the congested airports, she didn't set the high electricity rates that make hotels expensive, and she didn't create the archipelago geography that makes travel difficult.

If you replaced Frasco tomorrow with the most competent, politically savvy tourism secretary in the world, the airports would still be congested, domestic flights would still be expensive, and Chinese tourists would still be hesitant to visit a country that is in a standoff with their government.

But would a different secretary avoid a PHP 49 million campaign with foreign footage? Probably. Would they avoid making themselves the face of every tourism banner? Likely.

So Frasco is a problem, but not the problem. She has made the optics worse and given critics easy targets, but the real barriers to Philippine tourism success lie much deeper than a tourism secretary's performance.

## The Structural Problem: Philippine Tourism's Competitive Disadvantage

This is the part that hurts: even if DOT had a perfect slogan and a flawless secretary, Philippine tourism would still be struggling.

## Infrastructure: The First And Last Impressions Are Bad

Analysts and travel writers describe a common experience: it is harder, slower, and more stressful to get to and around the Philippines than it is in Thailand or Vietnam.

Common complaints include:
• NAIA is congested and outdated. Arriving there gives a poor first impression.
• Inter-island travel is messy. You often need multiple flights plus boats just to reach a single destination.
• Domestic flights are prone to delays and cancellations. A typhoon or even light bad weather can trigger a chain reaction of cancellations.
• Internet and mobile connectivity are weak in many places, even in Metro Manila, which matters a lot to modern travelers.

The result: traveling around the Philippines feels like a project. Meanwhile, going to Bangkok, Bali, or Da Nang feels like a vacation.

## The Cost Paradox: Domestic Travel Is More Expensive

Another painful aspect: traveling within the Philippines is often more expensive than traveling abroad.

Examples:
• A trip to Siargao can cost more than a trip to Hong Kong, Bangkok, or Vietnam, according to Bianca Tan of the Cebu Chamber of Commerce.
• Manila–El Nido flight: around PHP 14,000–16,000 roundtrip.
• Manila–Siargao flight: around PHP 7,000–14,000 roundtrip.
• A night in a Batangas resort: PHP 5,500–10,000 for mid-range quality.

On top of that, international airfare is often heavily discounted through promos and flash sales. Domestic flights within the Philippines rarely see the same deep discounts because there is less competition and higher operating costs.

Because of logistics costs, fuel, limited competition on certain routes, and uneven service standards, the Philippines is a high-cost destination with a middle-range infrastructure. That is a bad combination for growth.

## How Our Neighbors Are Doing

Here is the rough picture for 2025. Thailand pulled in around 32.9 million tourists. Malaysia had around 28 million. Vietnam hit 15.5 million. The Philippines, at 6.48 million, is not even in the same conversation.

Our neighbors are not just selling pretty beaches or nice views. They are selling ease of entry, cheap flights, fast trains, and a high-value experience for every dollar spent. We are selling a high-stress, high-cost experience with beautiful views at the end.

## ASEAN Chairmanship: A Missed Chance Or An Opening?

The Philippines is chairing ASEAN in 2026, hosting tourism meetings and bigger regional events. This is supposed to be our big moment to show off. But if the main headlines are about visa-free Chinese tourists and political infighting over the DOT secretary, we are missing the chance to talk about the reforms we actually need.

## The Economic Context: Why Tourism Matters More Than Ever

Tourism is not just about "fun" or "branding." It is one of the sectors keeping the economy afloat.

The Philippines is expected to have missed its GDP growth target again in 2025. Growth is likely around 5.5 to 5.7 percent, well below the 6.5 to 7.5 percent target.

That means less money moving through the system, slower job creation, and more pressure on remittances from OFWs. Tourism is one of the few sectors that can create millions of jobs across different skill levels, from hotel staff to boatmen to local guides.

So when the government pushes for policies like visa-free entry for Chinese tourists, it is partly an act of economic desperation. They need the numbers to fill the gap left by missing growth targets elsewhere.

## Answering The Core Questions

Let me go back to the questions at the start of this whole thing.

## Is The Chinese Tourist Push A Concession?

On paper, the 14-day visa-free policy is not a direct surrender to China. It is limited to one year, restricted to two airports, and comes with security conditions.

But the context makes it feel like a concession. Chinese vessels are still harassing our ships and fishing boats. Chinese officials still issue statements that challenge our rights in the West Philippine Sea. Then suddenly, we make it easier for Chinese nationals to enter the country at a time when emotions are high.

So in design, it is economic and practical. In timing and perception, it feels more like a political gamble. It will only turn into a true concession if problems appear—illegal activities, security issues, POGO-like patterns—and the government refuses to adjust or cancel the policy. That is the real test.

## Has This Administration Learned From Duterte's China Problem?

In some important ways, yes. It walked away from China's Belt and Road promises. It finally killed POGOs. It strengthened ties with the U.S. and other allies. It speaks more bluntly and consistently about defending the West Philippine Sea.

But in other ways, it is still caught in the same trap: we need Chinese trade and Chinese tourists, and that need softens how far we are willing to push back in other arenas.

The difference is that under Duterte, the tilt toward China was sold as a "new golden age" with very little transparency. Under Marcos, the tilt is smaller, more cautious, and still tries to hold on to alliances with the U.S., Japan, and others. But the balancing act is fragile.

## Are Chinese Tourists The Only Focus?

No. The data makes that clear. South Korea is our biggest market. The U.S. is stable and large. Japan, Australia, Canada, India, and the Middle East are all important growth or focus areas.

DOT's 2026 plan is to recover South Koreans, deepen the U.S. and Canada markets, grow India and halal/Muslim-friendly tourism, and use the restored PHP 1 billion budget more aggressively.

Chinese tourists are just one piece of that puzzle. They are getting a lot of attention right now because of geopolitics, not because they are the majority of visitors.

## Final Thoughts: Pragmatism, Risk, And What Really Needs Fixing

So where does this leave us?

The visa-free policy for Chinese tourists is a tactical move inside a bigger mess. It tries to bring in more money and visitors in a tough year. It has limits, it has security conditions, and it mirrors what our neighbors are doing. On paper, it is pragmatic. In reality, it is risky because of timing, public distrust, and our bad experience with POGOs and Duterte's quiet deals.

The Marcos administration has fixed some of Duterte's worst China mistakes. It is more assertive in the West Philippine Sea, more open to allies, and less dependent on Chinese loans. But it still has not articulated a full, clear strategy for tourism that goes beyond short-term arrivals and slogans.

Frasco is not the only problem. The entire tourism system—from airports to ferries to pricing to safety—is dragging us down. When domestic travel costs more than flying to another country, and when it is harder to reach Siargao than Bali, people will vote with their wallets. No slogan can fix that.

The hardest truth is this: we can welcome Chinese tourists, Koreans, Americans, or anyone else, but they won't save us if we don't fix the product.

Because in the end, if the product is broken, it does not matter who we invite in. They will come once, see the mess, and never return.

The Fear That Became My Guide

It’s strange how certain fears can shape our lives, emerging from the simplest of moments. I was 17 at the kryptē — a quiet chapel below the main church at Christ the King Seminary in Quezon City. I had gone there for some peace and meditation, but I wasn’t alone. Father Rudolf Horst, SVD, was there, deep in prayer. After a while, he looked up, noticed me, and smiled. That small gesture led to a conversation I’ve carried with me ever since.

Fr. Rudolf and I became friends because I would serve as an altar server for him during the Mass at St. Paul University. The man was always deep in thought, serene, and full of wisdom. In that quiet kryptē, his presence felt almost grounding. With calm certainty, he said something that would stay with me for years: “Do the right thing, even when no one is watching. Remember this, character is what you are in the dark.”

At that age, it felt like a heavy responsibility. I could only imagine what it meant to live up to such a standard. I wasn’t the ideal seminarian, at least not in the eyes of my batchmates and priests, but I loved being there because, for the first time in many years, I felt “home.” That sense of belonging filled something within me, even if my path wasn’t destined to stay there.

That same lesson resurfaced years later through a friend from Naga City who gave me a book. Inside the cover, they had written nearly the same message: “Do the right thing even when no one’s watching.” I’d been reminded once again — almost as if fate wanted this principle engraved into my heart.

## Not All Mistakes Are Actually Mistakes

For many years, I believed in the importance of this fear — a quiet, constant worry about doing what was right. But despite that fear, I made my share of choices that didn’t feel right at the time. One of the biggest was leaving the seminary at the age of twenty. At the time, it felt like an unanswered question, a turning point I couldn’t quite reconcile. I spent years wondering if I’d left something sacred behind, questioning whether I’d failed.

But life has a way of turning certain detours into blessings. When I got married at thirty-four, I realized that if I hadn’t left, I wouldn’t have met my incredible wife, and I wouldn’t be a father to my wonderful daughter. What I’d considered a misstep led me to a life I wouldn’t trade for anything. Not every regret is truly a mistake; many are just part of a journey that we can’t fully understand until later.

## Despite the Fear, Tons of Mistakes — and Not-Mistakes

The funny thing is, even with Father Rudolf’s wisdom guiding me, I still made choices that felt like failures. Some were small and forgettable, like getting too caught up in work to make it to family gatherings. Others were more significant — like taking a job I thought would be fulfilling, only to discover it wasn’t the right fit. For a while, these felt like failures, weights I would carry.

Yet, as time passed, I began to see some of these so-called “wrong” choices as doors to something unexpected. Each “misstep” felt less like a setback and more like an invitation to grow. Looking back, what I once called mistakes were sometimes just different roads, leading me to lessons and people I needed to encounter. Winston Churchill’s words often come to mind: “Success is not final, failure is not fatal: it is the courage to continue that counts.” Over the years, I’ve learned that it’s less about avoiding mistakes and more about having the courage to keep moving, keep learning, and, when necessary, keep forgiving myself.

## Lessons Learned: Humility and Growth

If there’s anything these experiences taught me, it’s humility. That initial fear of doing wrong has matured into something else — a deeper sense of accountability, shaped by the quiet endurance each path demanded. Integrity isn’t about perfection; it’s about staying true to yourself even when things don’t go as planned. Humility grew less from success and more from learning to stand back up after a fall, however quietly. I try to hold on to Father Rudolf’s words: that character is what you are in the dark. They still guide me, reminding me to keep trying, to apologize when I need to, and to embrace the journey, mistakes and all.

The Bible captures it well in Proverbs 16:9: “The heart of man plans his way, but the Lord establishes his steps.” Our paths may not always make sense in the moment, but in time, they reveal their purpose.

## Embracing the Journey

In the end, this fear — this quiet guide — has been more of a friend than an enemy. It’s nudged me to stay honest and to keep learning. Sitting here with my coffee, I realize that Father Rudolf’s words — to do the right thing even when no one is watching — have been with me all along. They don’t weigh me down; they keep me grounded, reminding me that every choice has meaning, even if it isn’t always clear at first.

While writing this, I grew curious about how Father Rudolf Horst was doing. I searched, hoping perhaps to find a recent sermon or a talk he’d given. Instead, I found something that made me pause: a funeral Mass held at Christ the King Seminary in his honor. Father Rudolf had passed, leaving behind the wisdom and grace he shared with so many.

The late Fr. Rudolf Horst, SVD
Requiescat in Pace, Fr. Rudy. May you rest in peace. Your words and kindness continue to guide those of us still here now — like a quiet echo, guiding in ways that words alone never could.

There’s something oddly humbling about waking up to mayors who just… said no. No to power they could’ve held onto without question. No to funds they could’ve used in silence. No to a system that keeps getting away with calling secrecy “security.”

In a country where public funds too often disappear without receipts, a few local leaders have chosen to give up confidential funds entirely. It shouldn’t be headline material. But it is. Because too many see public money as a privilege, not a responsibility. And that’s exactly why these mayors deserve our attention.

The First to Draw the Line
It started with former Vice President, now Naga City Mayor, Leni Robredo. During her inauguration, she said it plainly: “Primarily, we will remove confidential funds from our budget. So expect this to be gone.” ([GMA News](https://www.gmanetwork.com/news/topstories/regions/950983/ex-vp-turned-mayor-leni-robredo-vows-to-remove-confi-funds-in-naga-budget/story/)) She didn’t soften the statement or load it with excuses. It wasn’t framed as cost-cutting or reform—it was a refusal to be part of a system that allows public money to move in the dark.

Soon after, others followed. Dumanjug Mayor Efren “Gungun” Gica announced: “Effective immediately, no more confidential funds for LGU Dumanjug. Public funds must serve the people clearly, and with integrity.” ([GMA News](https://www.gmanetwork.com/news/topstories/nation/951289/lawmaker-confidential-funds-should-be-regulated-not-banned/story/))

Capas, Tarlac Mayor Roseller “Boots” Rodriguez did the same—giving up ₱8 million in confidential funds to instead fund assistance for senior citizens and school supplies for students. He said: “To my colleagues and fellow elected officials, I set this example. I hope you follow the leader. We don’t have to compromise, but sometimes we must sacrifice for those who trusted us.” ([Inquirer](https://newsinfo.inquirer.net/2080766/reelected-tarlac-town-mayor-gives-up-p8-m-confidential-fund))

This isn’t budgeting. It’s leadership.

Why It Matters
These mayors didn’t do it to impress. They didn’t hold concerts or make TikTok-friendly PR moves. They just closed the backdoor from their budgets. That’s what confidential funds are, after all—money you can spend with zero oversight. No need to justify anything. No clear rules. No oversight. No paper trail.

And while some argue these funds are essential for peace and order or anti-crime work, that logic has been stretched beyond its original intent. Especially when Vice President Sara Duterte reportedly spent ₱125 million in just 11 days ([PhilStar](https://www.philstar.com/headlines/2023/12/28/2321853/yearender-coa-exposes-dutertes-predilection-confidential-funds)). And those funds? They were disbursed to names like Mary Grace Piattos and Kokoy Villamin—recipients that, by any reasonable standard, don’t pass the sniff test ([Inquirer](https://newsinfo.inquirer.net/2080793/if-vp-sara-addressed-secret-fund-use-issue-earlier-is-done-chua)).

That’s not security. That’s fiction.

Meanwhile, Davao City under her mayoralty spent ₱2.697 billion in confidential funds over several years ([PhilStar](https://www.philstar.com/headlines/2023/10/02/2300607/enough-confidential-fund-spree-teachers)). To put it in perspective, that money could have paid ₱5,000 annual allowances to 77,000 teachers for six straight years. Now compare that to a mayor in Capas giving up ₱8 million so seniors can get help and kids can have school supplies. That contrast says more than any editorial ever could.

The Irony No One’s Saying Out Loud
Let’s not pretend this movement started organically. It didn’t. It was triggered by a bad example. A spectacularly arrogant one. The mayors who are giving up confidential funds are, in many ways, reacting to Sara Duterte’s abuse of it. Not in direct confrontation, but through contrast. They're showing what public service is supposed to look like by highlighting how broken it has become under her watch.

This is a slap—not just to the misuse of funds—but to the entitlement that comes with it. Because Sara didn’t just spend public money recklessly. She refused to be questioned. She didn’t say “I am a Duterte,” but she didn’t have to. The message was clear in her posture, in her silence, in the way she flipped the script and played the victim when people tried to follow the tracks.

No remorse. No transparency. No explanation. Just indifference.

That’s not governance. That’s a display of impunity—and these mayors are quietly tearing it down.

More Than Policy—It’s a Signal
Rodriguez’s words weren’t just a statement—they were a call: “I hope you follow the leader.” Because when one mayor does it, others realize they can, too. And just like that, the “default setting” changes.

Confidential funds shouldn’t be routine. They should be rare, strictly used, and heavily accounted for. Instead, we’ve normalized them across agencies and local governments—many of which have no business handling intelligence funds to begin with. Even the Commission on Audit has asked for stricter guidelines ([COA](https://www.coa.gov.ph/wpfd_file/guidelines-on-confidential-and-intelligence-fund-use-signed/)), and lawmakers have called for gradual abolition or regulation in agencies where they don’t belong ([GMA](https://www.gmanetwork.com/news/topstories/nation/934853/bondoc-removal-of-confidential-intelligence-funds-on-agencies-should-be-gradual/story/)).

In a Country Tired of Excuses
This kind of leadership—quiet, principled, clear-eyed—isn’t flashy. And that’s probably why it doesn’t get enough airtime. But it should. Because for every mayor who gives up confidential funds, there’s a whole system that gets nudged—however slightly—toward integrity. Every peso redirected to school kids or seniors is one less peso waiting to be buried in “discretionary expenses.”

Will Others Follow?
That’s the real question now. Will more LGUs follow suit? Or will the rest of them pretend they need confidential funds to “maintain peace and order,” even as we wonder: peace and order for whom?

These mayors aren’t just saving money. They’re exposing the lie that transparency is optional. They’re proving that a mayor can lead effectively without secret funds—and still sleep at night knowing every peso is accounted for. In a time when corruption is too often passed off as “just the way things work,” they’re proving that integrity actually means something.

No Hashtag, No Drama—Just Trust
What we’re witnessing isn’t just responsible governance. It’s a slow but powerful shift in how we define power. Less about speeches. More about receipts. Less about clout. More about choices.

And maybe that’s why it feels so refreshing. Because in a country where too many in power hold on tight to what they can hide, it’s nice to know there are still leaders who choose what they can give up.

Struggling to land clients in freelancing? Don’t give up yet. Learn from a personal journey of overcoming dry spells, mastering negotiations, and building a reputation that attracts clients instead of chasing them.

You’re a freelancer, but you’re not getting any clients. Don’t give up just yet. Let me tell you my story.

When I first started freelancing, Upwork was still called Odesk. I was working in a call center with a decent salary, but the idea of working from home felt like a golden opportunity I couldn’t pass up. I thought, “I’ll just set up a profile, and the clients will start flooding in.” Spoiler alert: They didn’t.

I waited. And waited. Crickets.

## The Reality Check

I was listing my skills like a resume, hoping someone would take a chance on me. Customer service, data entry, basic writing—I thought I was covering all the bases. But nothing happened. No bites. It wasn’t until I stumbled across some advice online that I realized my big mistake. I had no idea what **profile optimization** meant. I was just throwing everything at the wall, hoping something would stick.

I learned the hard way that your freelancing profile isn’t just a resume—it’s a pitch. You’re writing for two audiences: the algorithm (Upwork is a search engine, after all) and the client. If you don’t use the right keywords, nobody’s finding you. If you don’t sound like a human, not a robot, nobody’s hiring you either.

## Shifting the Mindset

Once I figured that out, I rewrote my profile. I went from a generic list of skills to positioning myself as a writer—a skill I had almost forgotten I had experience in. Back in the '90s, I used to write essays and articles for classmates for a few bucks. Why not lean into that skill?

But even then, I had another hurdle. I was approaching clients like a job hunter, eager to please and willing to do anything. I realized that clients don’t want an order-taker. They want a consultant—someone who’s confident in their work and can guide them through the process. So I started asking better questions, like: *What’s your target word count? How many blogs are we writing? Are you providing the keywords? What’s the main goal of this content—traffic, sales, or brand awareness?*

## Low Rates and Self-Doubt

In the beginning, I charged $5 for a thousand words. Yeah, you heard that right—$5. I thought lower prices would make me irresistible, but it didn’t. Clients still hesitated, and I realized it wasn’t about the price. The problem was trust. I wasn’t presenting myself as an expert, and it showed.

## Building Trust with a Public Portfolio

I knew I needed to prove my legitimacy, so I started a blog on WordPress. It wasn’t fancy. Honestly, I didn’t know what I was doing. I knew nothing about SEO back then. But it was a start. I kept writing and sharing my posts on Facebook. It wasn’t just about showing off my work; it was about letting clients see that I was a real person, a writer who published regularly, not just someone hiding behind closed doors.

That transparency paid off. Potential clients could see my consistency, and it built trust. They weren’t just hiring a ghost; they were hiring someone they felt they already knew.

## The ChatGPT Experiment

Fast forward to the launch of ChatGPT 3.5. A lot of my clients decided to test the waters with AI-generated content to save money. And they did—for a while. But soon, they started coming back. The content was flat, lifeless, and repetitive. They realized what I’ve known for a while: AI can generate words, but it can’t replicate the human experience and connection that real writing brings.

## From Pitching to Referrals

These days, I don’t send out proposals anymore. I’m loaded with work. It’s a complete 180 from scraping the bottom for clients. Now, they’re finding me. I get referrals and direct invitations because my reputation speaks for itself. But it wasn’t always like this. I sent out hundreds of proposals in the early days, hustling hard for every gig.

## Looking Ahead: Scaling the Right Way

I’ve thought about hiring help, but if I do, it’s going to be through an agency model. It’s about ethics. Clients who hire me expect my writing, my voice. Handing off work without transparency feels like a betrayal. If I’m going to scale, it’s going to be the right way: by training and mentoring someone who I can vouch for.

## Don’t Give Up Just Yet

Listen, I know it’s hard. I know what it’s like to feel like you’re shouting into the void, waiting for someone to give you a chance. But if you stick it out, keep refining your approach, and show up consistently, things can change. It took me years, but I’m living proof that persistence pays off.

The next client could be just an email away.

In my freelancing journey, I've seen how quickly the landscape evolves, with new trends and tools shaping how we work and earn a living. This shift is changing how freelancers and clients build relationships. It's exciting to see new opportunities opening up, especially ones that promote long-term collaborations instead of one-off projects.

One such trend gaining traction is freelancing subscriptions, a model where freelancers offer their services on a recurring basis for a fixed monthly or yearly fee. This shift from one-time gigs to ongoing client relationships presents a unique opportunity for freelancers to build stability, predictability, and potentially higher earning potential.

## How It Works: Setting Up and Managing Freelancing Subscriptions

Freelancing subscriptions offer a structured approach to ongoing client relationships, making it easier for both freelancers and clients to manage deliverables and payments. Here's a detailed look at how this model works, including a sample pricing table to illustrate its flexibility.

## Step-by-Step Guide for Freelancers

1. Identify Services Suited for Subscriptions: Choose services that clients need regularly, such as content creation, social media management, graphic design, or virtual assistance. These should be tasks that offer consistent value.
2. Create Subscription Tiers: Design tiered packages with varying levels of service. For example:
   - Basic Tier: Minimal services for budget-conscious clients.
   - Standard Tier: Mid-level services for clients with moderate needs.
   - Premium Tier: Comprehensive services for clients requiring extensive support.
   - Enterprise: Custom plans tailored to specific client requirements.
3. Set Pricing: Determine fair pricing based on the value you provide, your expertise, and market demand. Consider offering discounts for longer-term subscriptions.
4. Select a Platform or Setup: Decide where to host your subscription services, such as freelancing platforms like Upwork, or your own website with payment processing tools.
5. Draft a Clear Agreement: Define the scope of work, payment terms, cancellation policies, and other essential details in a contract or service agreement.

## Step-by-Step Guide for Clients

1. Search for Subscription-Based Services: Use freelancing platforms or networks to find freelancers offering subscription models.
2. Evaluate Tiers and Packages: Review the pricing, deliverables, and freelancer reputation before committing to a plan.
3. Communicate Expectations: Clearly outline your needs to ensure the subscription plan aligns with your business goals.
4. Monitor Progress: Stay in touch with your freelancer to ensure deliverables meet your expectations.

## Types of Freelancing Subscriptions and Services Offered

Freelancing subscriptions cover many services, and many of these resonate strongly with my work. These include:

- Website Maintenance: Ongoing updates, security checks, and performance optimization for a monthly fee.
- Content Creation: Subscription packages for blog posts, articles, social media content, or other written materials.
- Social Media Management: Monthly subscriptions for managing social media accounts, creating content calendars, and running campaigns.
- Virtual Assistance: Administrative tasks, email management, scheduling, and other support functions.
- Graphic Design: A set number of design requests, such as social media graphics, website banners, or marketing materials.
- Technology & Programming: Logo design, website development, and WordPress development.
- Writing & Translation: Content writing, SEO article writing, and copy typing.

As these examples show, the subscription model is proving increasingly versatile, reaching into more and more services and industries.

## Platforms Offering Freelancing Subscriptions

While exploring the idea of freelancing subscriptions, I noticed that several platforms are starting to embrace this model. I found it intriguing because it seems to enhance collaboration between freelancers and clients, creating opportunities for stronger, more productive relationships.

- Freelancer.com: This platform offers a range of membership plans with varying levels of benefits, including increased bids, project upgrades, and access to premium features.
- Guru: Guru offers various membership options for freelancers, with paid plans providing benefits such as reduced service fees, more bids, and enhanced profile visibility.
- DesignHill: DesignHill offers a graphic design subscription service where businesses can subscribe to a monthly plan to receive unlimited graphic design services.

## Upwork: A Closer Look

Upwork, one of the most popular freelancing platforms, offers a variety of features to support freelancing subscriptions. As an Upwork freelancer, I was particularly interested in exploring their subscription-based payment models. If implemented effectively, this feature could allow freelancers like me to set recurring rates for ongoing projects, providing a more stable income stream.

## Pricing Models for Freelancing Subscriptions

Pricing subscription services requires careful thought, especially when considering how they align with your business goals.

- Tiered Pricing: Offering different subscription tiers with varying levels of service, features, or support.
- Value-Based Pricing: Setting prices based on the value delivered to the client rather than time spent.
- Fixed Monthly/Yearly Fee: Charging a set fee for a predetermined scope of work or number of service requests.

## Managing Subscriptions (For Freelancers)

1. Track Deliverables: Use tools like Trello or ClickUp to stay organized and ensure timely delivery.
2. Communicate Regularly: Maintain open communication with clients to address concerns or adjust plans as needed.
3. Handle Scope Creep: Reinforce boundaries and revise contracts if clients request additional work outside the subscription agreement.
4. Ensure Payment Security: Use reliable payment systems to avoid delays or issues with payments.

## Scope Creep: The Freelancer's Hidden Challenge

Of all the cons I see here, I realized that it's scope creep that needs focus. Scope creep is a sneaky problem that can gradually erode your profitability and work-life balance.

## What Scope Creep Looks Like

- Increasing Word Count Without Discussion: Agreeing to a specific word count, but the client keeps asking for more without adjusting the fee.
- More Interviews, More Problems: A client requesting additional interviews or research beyond the initial agreement.
- Endless Revisions: Going beyond the agreed number of revision rounds.
- Unscheduled Add-ons: A client asking for extra tasks that weren't part of the original maintenance or service package.

## How to Prevent and Manage Scope Creep

1. Define the Scope Clearly: Spell out every deliverable, timeline, and service at the start.
2. Communicate Changes Effectively: Respond professionally to additional requests by discussing how they affect the agreement.
3. Create a Change Control Process: For larger projects, implement a formal process for handling changes.
4. Educate the Client: Help clients understand the implications of additional requests.
5. Use Contracts and Agreements: A detailed contract is your best defense.
6. Upsell Strategically: Suggest upgrading to a higher service tier if requests are consistent.
7. Learn to Say No: It’s okay to decline unreasonable requests.

## Subscription-Based Freelancing: The Future of Work?

Freelancing subscriptions could change how we work, moving away from one-off projects to building long-term partnerships. While it might not work for everyone, it offers an exciting path toward stability and growth. As for me, I’m excited to give it a try and transform my approach to building a meaningful freelance career.

## Resetting the Government: A Risk The Philippines Can’t Afford

This reflection takes on the call for a complete reset — tearing down the Philippine government and starting over. It explains why corruption and inefficiency won’t vanish with a new form, since padrino politics, dynasties, and weak institutions always survive. It shows why collapse would crush the poor hardest, reminds us how close we came to the edge in 1986, and imagines what a shutdown would look like on the ground. Drawing from global state collapses and the dark forces still at work today, it calls out both leaders and citizens — warning that without steady reform and the courage to stop enabling corruption, the country risks sliding into a mafia state where gangsters take the place of government.

I’ve heard it too many times: “What if we change the system?” Some dream of parliament, some whisper about federalism, others even suggest scrapping democracy itself. The belief is always the same — that if we shift to a new form of government, the corruption and inefficiency will finally go away.

But moving rotten tomatoes into a new basket doesn’t make them fresh. It only changes the container. Corruption here isn’t a quirk of leadership or a matter of political form. It’s structural, baked into institutions that have been deliberately kept fragile so those in power can keep feeding off them. The padrino system, oligarchic capture, political dynasties, bureaucratic red tape — these don’t vanish when you change the shape of government. They adapt, survive, and flourish.

And when the frustration boils over, some argue that maybe it’s better to burn the whole thing down — dissolve the state, tear up the constitution, start from scratch. History has an answer for that, too: collapse punishes the poor first. So before we romanticize radical change, it’s worth asking: what really changes when the form of government shifts, and what happens when government disappears altogether?

## Institutional Foundation of Corruption

Corruption in the Philippines isn’t random. It’s a system — old, deliberate, and self-sustaining. Every time we elect new leaders, they don’t walk into a clean office. They inherit an entire operating system designed to favor connections over competence.

Take the padrino system. Born in the Spanish colonial period, it’s still the backbone of politics today. Appointments, promotions, and contracts aren’t handed out based on who is best for the job, but on who you know. Then there’s palakasan, the cultural cousin of padrino. It’s the “sino kilala mo sa loob” way of life. Everyone knows that to get things done, you don’t follow the rules; you find the right person to bypass them.

Over it all sits regulatory capture. Oligarchic families don’t just run businesses — they write the rules. From telecoms and shipping to energy and rice, the economy is carved up among players who use the state as a shield against competition. And then we get to political dynasties. More than 70% of elected seats are controlled by families who treat public office as business property, passed from one generation to the next.

If you need proof, look at how little has changed over the decades. Scandals rise, outrage follows, a few people are charged, and the public moves on. But the system itself remains intact. The names on the doors change. The networks don’t.

## Cultural and Systemic Entrenchment

Corruption isn’t just a game played at the top. It seeps into everyday life, shaping how ordinary people interact with authority. When the formal system is slow and broken, people look for shortcuts. This is the vicious cycle: people expect to use shortcuts, and officials expect to be paid for providing them.

Political dynasties deepen this entrenchment. For decades, families have passed down offices like inheritance, ensuring that local power structures remain loyal to the clan rather than the law. The impact isn’t abstract. Billions are poured into projects that fail the moment shovels hit the ground — flood control that doesn’t drain, roads that crumble after the first rain, and schools that are never finished. This is what keeps corruption alive: not only the grand scandals we see on the front pages but the quiet, daily siphoning of the country’s future.

## The Limits of Leadership-Centered Reform

Every change in leadership has carried the promise of renewal. Each time, the public is told that the problem is the person at the top. After the fall of Marcos, there was a sense that the country could reset itself. We had a new constitution, new leaders, and a new hope. But the institutions underneath remained weak.

Ramos had the PEA-Amari deal. Estrada fell under the weight of jueteng payoffs. Arroyo faced accusations of vote-rigging and the ZTE scandal. Benigno Aquino III ran on “Daang Matuwid,” but the DAP scandal and systemic corruption in the lower ranks of the bureaucracy showed that a clean leader at the top isn't enough to sanitize a dirty system. Then came Duterte, who built his campaign around the idea of “political will.” He projected strength, but corruption didn't vanish — it simply found new patrons and new channels.

The lesson is plain. Authoritarian strength doesn’t cure a system built on weak institutions. Strongmen often weaken institutions further by making them subservient to their own will. The focus on leadership, on finding the right “strong” figure to clean up the mess, misses the point. You cannot fix a house with a rotten foundation by just hiring a new gardener.

## The Budget and Economics of Corruption

Corruption is not only political; it is economic. It drains the very lifeblood of the country. Each year, it is estimated that billions, perhaps trillions of pesos, are lost to graft. These aren’t invisible losses. They show up in flooded streets where drainage projects exist only on paper. They show up in hospitals without medicine and schools without books.

The problem grows worse during elections. Campaigns in the Philippines are prohibitively expensive. Candidates spend fortunes to get elected, and once in power, their first priority is often to recoup that investment. This is how corruption becomes self-financing. The money stolen today funds the elections of tomorrow. When you put all this together — the trillions lost, the endless cycle of debt and repayment, the price ordinary citizens pay for broken services — you see the true cost of corruption. It isn’t just a moral failing. It is a theft of the country’s potential.

## Colonial Path Dependence

The corruption we live with today didn’t appear overnight. Its roots were planted centuries ago, and we have lived with the results ever since. Under Spanish rule, governance revolved around loyalty and privilege. The friar or the governor-general held absolute power, and the only way to survive was to gain their favor.

The Americans promised modernization, but much of what they built rested on the same fragile foundations. They institutionalized political power within the landed elite, creating the very dynasties that still rule today. When independence came, we inherited these institutions almost intact. We never tore them down or rebuilt them for a modern democracy. This is why corruption here feels permanent. It isn’t about who leads or what form of government we have. It’s about a path we started on hundreds of years ago and have never truly left.

## The Catastrophic Cost of Collapse

Some argue that the only way forward is to tear the whole thing down. Scrap democracy, dissolve the state, and start over from zero. They call it a “reset.” But the word “reset” sounds cleaner than the reality. When a state collapses, it doesn’t just restart like a computer. It dies.

### Somalia: The Failed State Blueprint
When Somalia’s government fell in 1991, the country lost more than just a president. Its economy shattered. Yes, remittances from the diaspora eventually kept the country on life support, but that doesn’t change the fact that an entire generation grew up without schools, hospitals, or the basic protection of the law.

### Yugoslavia: From Federation to Fragmentation
Yugoslavia’s story is one of economic collapse leading straight into bloody conflict. Hyperinflation reached trillions of percent. When the country dissolved, successor states faced severe economic decline and war. Infrastructure was obliterated, and the social fabric was torn apart.

### Soviet Union: Anticipation of Collapse as Collapse Itself
The Soviet Union collapsed not only when the flag came down, but in the years leading up to it. As the central government weakened, shortages became common. After the formal breakup, GDP across former Soviet states dropped by about 20%. Inflation hit triple digits. Life expectancy plummeted as the healthcare system disintegrated.

### Libya: Fast Freefall After Revolution
Libya in 2011 shows how quickly a modern economy can unravel when government collapses. GDP fell by over 60% in a single year as oil production plunged. Public services didn’t just weaken — they disappeared. A country once boasting some of Africa’s highest living standards descended into chaos, leaving citizens dependent on militia-controlled zones and fragmented governance.

### Afghanistan: Aid Cut, Society Shocked
When the Taliban took over in 2021, foreign aid — nearly half the country’s GDP — was cut off overnight. Within a year, the economy shrank by a third. Jobs vanished, businesses shut down, and up to 70% of Afghans could no longer afford basic necessities. For families who were already poor, the collapse meant hunger.

### Iraq: Institutions Dismantled, Recovery Stalled
Iraq’s collapse after 2003 illustrates how dismantling institutions creates wounds that don’t heal quickly. Two decades of sanctions had already weakened the economy, but when existing structures were torn down instead of reformed, instability deepened. Oil revenues became the only pillar left, making the country dangerously dependent on a single resource.

### Central African Republic: The Long Shadow of Collapse
The Central African Republic is another cautionary tale. Once government broke apart, recovery became almost impossible. Decades of conflict have left the economy in ruins.

### The Common Thread
Across these cases — Somalia, Yugoslavia, the Soviet Union, Libya, Afghanistan, Iraq, and the Central African Republic — the lesson is the same. Collapse is a tax on the poor. And they pay it every single day.

## What Collapse Would Look Like in the Philippines

Let’s assume, for a moment, that we take the route of a total reset. The government is shut down completely. Picture payday morning. Workers line up at ATMs across the country, but the machines flash the same message: system offline. Banks have locked their doors. The peso has no anchor. Those with savings watch them turn to dust as the currency loses value in hours.

At gas stations, the queues stretch for blocks. By evening, the pumps run dry. Jeepneys stop plying their routes because they can no longer find or afford fuel. Night falls and the power cuts. Whole barangays are swallowed by darkness. In Manila, the skyline turns into a skeleton of unlit towers.

Supermarkets don’t last a week. Shelves stripped bare, checkout counters abandoned. In the wet markets, vendors trade only in barter or foreign cash. Law and order unravels in plain sight. Mayors and governors retreat to their compounds, flanked by private armies. In the vacuum, gangs take over the streets, charging residents for “protection” or food.

For the poor, there is no escape. Hunger sets in first, followed by disease. Parents skip meals so their children can eat, but soon there is nothing left to skip. If this sounds far-fetched, remember the pandemic. With just partial government paralysis, millions were pushed to the brink. In a total collapse, there is no one to distribute aid. And unlike a storm, collapse doesn’t pass. It settles. This is the reset imagined as liberation — but lived as ruin.

## Why Collapse Would Hit the Philippines Harder

History gives us examples of collapse, but the Philippines carries its own unique weaknesses that would make a reset even more dangerous. We should remember that we’ve already lived through one reset. In 1986, people filled EDSA and forced a dictator out. We were fortunate that democracy held, fragile as it was. That kind of luck doesn't come twice.

When people call for another reset, they speak as if it would automatically lead to something better. But today, the dark forces embedded in our politics are stronger, richer, and more prepared to seize opportunity if the state falters. Now add the vulnerabilities unique to the Philippines. We are not a compact landmass like Libya or Iraq. We are 7,000 islands. In a collapse, the central authority would vanish, and the country would fragment into fiefdoms ruled by warlords and dynasties.

The economy is also tightly tied to global trust. Our lifeline — the billions in overseas remittances — depends on a functioning banking system. Foreign investors would vanish overnight. Exports would stop moving. And because the Philippines sits on the front lines of global geopolitical tension, a collapse would invite foreign powers to step in, not to help us, but to secure their own interests.

Politically, collapse doesn’t erase patronage. It mutates it. The same dynasties that thrive today would be the ones with the private armies and stockpiled supplies tomorrow. The result would not be freedom. It would be a return to feudalism, this time dressed as warlordism. For a country as fragmented, dependent, and strategically located as the Philippines, collapse would not be a new beginning. It would be the end of the nation as we know it.

## Reform Within the System

If collapse is ruin and resets are illusions, then what remains is the long, difficult road of reform. It is not as exciting as a revolution, and it doesn't offer the instant gratification of a “clean slate.” But it is the only path that works.

Reform begins with professionalizing the civil service. Government posts cannot remain rewards for political loyalty. They must be filled by people who are protected from the whims of whichever family is in power. Processes also need to be stripped of their loopholes. As long as permits, licenses, and government services remain complicated, corruption will find a home in the shadows.

Judicial independence must be strengthened. Without courts that can stand up to political pressure, laws are just suggestions. Campaign finance reform is another pillar. As long as elections cost billions, candidates will remain beholden to the interests that fund them. And yet, reform demands something harder than laws and policies. It requires institutions that choose to follow those laws, even when it is inconvenient. This is the work of decades, not days. But reform, slow as it is, remains the only option that does not end in ruin.

## Doable Now: Small Gears

Big reforms sound overwhelming, almost impossible. But even small gears can move a machine. Transparency is one. Every contract, every project, every peso spent should be visible to the public. Digitization is another. Automating basic services — licenses, permits, tax filings — cuts out the middleman and the opportunity for grease money.

Civic pressure matters too. Protests and public outcry may not always topple dynasties, but they create a cost for corruption. Partnerships with watchdogs, both local and international, can help. Technical support, monitoring, and regular audits can catch leakage before it becomes a flood. These aren’t silver bullets. They don’t erase dynasties or padrino politics overnight. But they tilt the field in favor of the honest and the capable.

## The Role of the People

Corruption doesn’t survive on its own. It survives because people live inside it. Every shortcut taken, every bribe paid, every pakisuyo to someone inside the system keeps it alive. Ordinary citizens aren’t the ones who designed it, but we are the ones who fuel it.

That doesn’t mean blame belongs to the people. When the honest path is slower, costlier, and more painful, it’s only natural for people to look for another way. This creates the cultural resignation of “ganyan na talaga.” The same truth applies to elections. Dynasties keep winning not only because they are powerful, but because people feel they have no other choice.

But the power to change that choice rests with the people. Small refusals matter. Refusing to sell a vote. Refusing to pay grease money. Refusing to look away when a public project is clearly being plundered. These small acts of defiance, when multiplied by millions, create a climate where corruption can no longer breathe. Institutions can change, but only when citizens demand it. Without pressure from below, those at the top will never move.

## Where I Stand

Changing the form of government will not clean up corruption. Dissolving the state will not deliver justice. Collapse punishes the poor first and hardest, while dynasties and cartels adapt to whatever system replaces the old one.

We’ve already tried a reset once in 1986. We were fortunate that democracy held, fragile as it was. That kind of luck doesn’t come twice. Today, the dark forces embedded in our politics are stronger, richer, and more prepared to seize opportunity if the state falters.

The real work is slower. It’s the grind of reform — professionalizing the bureaucracy, protecting the courts, cutting down campaign costs, and dragging every transaction into the open. It’s not as dramatic as a revolution, and it won’t make headlines overnight. But it’s the only path that doesn’t end with a mafia state running the country.

So when people talk about resetting the system or shifting to another form of government, I go back to that simple picture: rotten tomatoes in a new basket. The container changes, the decay remains. The harder path is keeping the basket we have and finally removing the tomatoes that poison it.
